title: Chapter 19: The House Call
content: The navy blazer fits like armor.

I check myself in the mirror for the third time. Black turtleneck, straight-leg trousers, hair pulled back tight enough to hurt. I look like a paralegal. I look like someone who shows up to a law office with a folder full of evidence and doesn't leave until someone listens. Which is exactly the vibe I'm going for.

I've rehearsed it six times. The opening. The pivot. The ask. Mom, I signed a contract I didn't understand, and now it's taking pieces of me, and I need you to help me find a way out. Clean. Professional. No tears. No begging. Just the facts, presented to a woman who respects nothing if not facts.

Leo's at the kitchen counter, running another search on Icelandic contract law precedents. His monitors bathe the apartment in that familiar glow. The red string on the wall has grown — new connections, new printouts, a web of desperation that would make a conspiracy theorist blush.
"Your mom's office is on Fifty-second, right?" Leo says without looking up. "You should leave in ten if you want to be early."
"I know."
"You rehearsed the pitch?"
"I said I know, Leo."
He holds up both hands. "Okay. Okay. Just — you've got this."

I grab my bag. Check for the folder. The printed contract. The timeline I've written out in longhand because something about pen on paper feels more real, more like evidence, more like something a lawyer would respect. My phone buzzes — Mom's assistant confirming the 2PM. I slide it into my pocket.
Deep breath. I'm ready.
I open the front door.
Mephisto is standing there.

Not on the intercom. Not downstairs in the lobby charming the doorman. Right here. On my welcome mat. Like he materialized out of the wallpaper, out of the dead air between seconds, out of whatever dark geometry sustains things like him.

He's wearing a charcoal suit that probably costs more than my rent. His tie is the color of dried blood — elegant, understated, the kind of color choice a stylist makes when they want to say threat without screaming it. His hands are clasped in front of him. Relaxed. Patient.
He smiles.
"Going somewhere?" he asks. Like he's asking about the weather. Like he's asking if I've tried the new coffee shop on the corner.
My hand is still on the door. My knuckles have gone white. I can taste copper at the back of my throat — adrenaline, the body's default setting for when reality faults.
"You're in my hallway."
"I am." He tilts his head. "May I come in?"

It's not a question. I can tell because he's already moving, and the word no dies somewhere between my brain and my mouth because some part of me — the part that signed the contract, the part that said yes please take what you need — already agreed to let him in a long time ago.
He steps past me into my apartment.
He's never been inside before.

I realize this with a lurch of violation so intense it almost knocks me sideways. Every other encounter has been on neutral ground — his office, a restaurant, that rooftop bar where he told me the deductions would begin. My apartment has been mine. Safe. The one square of Manhattan that didn't have his fingerprints on it.
And now he's looking around.

His gaze moves the way a lender's does — appraising, cataloging, marking value. The exposed brick. The secondhand couch. Leo's monitors on the kitchen counter, still cycling through Icelandic legal databases. The red string on the wall, trailing from one pinned document to the next.

His expression doesn't change. But something behind his eyes flickers. Not anger. Not surprise. Interest. The way a chess player notices an unexpected move — not threatening, exactly, but worth recalibrating for.
"Cozy," he says. "Very... collaborative."
The word hits like a slap. Collaborative. He sees the string. He sees the monitors. He sees Leo, who's standing now, hand frozen over his keyboard, face gone tight and pale.
Mephisto walks to my couch. Sits down. Crosses his legs.

MY couch. The one I bought off Facebook Marketplace from a woman in Queens who was getting divorced and selling everything. The one where I've eaten takeout and cried over movies I can no longer feel and fallen asleep with my head on Leo's shoulder during late-night research binges. He sits on it like he's settling into a boardroom chair, and some small, feral part of me wants to scream.

The cold hits me then — not the apartment, not the January draft. Him. His presence drops the temperature three degrees, like he's absorbing the heat from the room, from me, from everything he touches. I can smell him: oud and something older underneath. Something beneath civilization. Old stone and old air, the way cathedrals smell in countries that forgot they built them.
"Miss Hart." He folds his hands on his knee. "Did you think we wouldn't notice?"
I don't answer. My silence is the only weapon I have and I'm gripping it with both hands.

"The unusual data patterns," he continues, as if I'd asked. "The midnight server-pinging. The decompiled contract fragments in your associate's cache." He pauses. Lets it land. Then he turns his head toward Leo.
"Mr. Park."
Leo flinches. Actually flinches, like the name physically touched him.
"MIT dropout. Impressive algorithmic intuition for a self-taught developer." Mephisto's smile is a crack in a porcelain mask. "Pity about the student loans."
He knows Leo's name.
He knows Leo's history.
He knows more about Leo than *I* do, and I've spent the last three weeks sleeping twenty feet from him. The realization lands like a stone in water — heavy, deep, rippling outward into dark.
"How —" Leo starts.

"How did we know?" Mephisto doesn't even look at him. "Mr. Park. Our data infrastructure processes four hundred thousand contracts simultaneously. Did you imagine your browsing history presented a particular challenge?"
He turns back to me. Those eyes — not quite blue, not quite anything — catch the light from Leo's monitors and flicker. A cold electric shimmer, server-light on deep water.

"I understand you have an appointment today," he says. Conversationally. Mildly. "With your mother. Evelyn Hart. Partners at Whitfield, Crane & Associates. Twenty-three years of IP law." A beat. "Currently being considered for senior partnership."
The copper taste floods my mouth again. My hands are shaking. I clench them at my sides.
"It would be a shame," Mephisto says, "if something were to... complicate that consideration."
There it is.

No threat. Nothing you could quote back to a lawyer, nothing you could screenshot and label EXHIBIT A. Just a man on a couch, speaking in the passive voice, letting the architecture of implication do the work. He's not threatening my mother's career. He's just *mentioning* it. Mentioning that it exists. Mentioning that it's fragile. Mentioning that the distance between "being considered for senior partnership" and "no longer being considered" is the width of a well-placed word.
Corporate speak as weapon. Every sentence polished smooth into something you can't grab, can't prove, can't fight.
"Are you threatening my mother?"
The words come out steadier than I expect. A small victory.

Mephisto blinks. The performance of surprise. "Miss Hart. I would never." He places a hand on his chest — the gesture of a man who's been deeply, *personally* offended. "I'm merely observing that the legal profession is, shall we say, sensitive to reputational disruption. And that our contract, should it become a matter of public record, would constitute a rather... unusual narrative."

He lets me fill in the rest. My daughter signed away her emotions to a corporation. She's claiming supernatural contract enforcement. She's seeking foreign legal counsel to challenge an agreement she entered willingly. How would partners at a white-shoe firm view that? How fast would "senior partnership consideration" become "we think it's best you take some time"?
He doesn't have to do anything. The threat is self-executing. It's a clause built into the architecture of respectability — and he wrote it before I was born.
Leo moves.

I see it in peripheral: him pushing back from the counter, standing, crossing the room in three strides. He plants himself between me and Mephisto, and for one insane second I think he's going to hit him — my gentle, caffeinated, anxiety-spiraling Leo is going to throw a punch at a supernatural entity in a ten-thousand-dollar suit.
"Get out of her apartment."
His voice cracks on the last word. Not fear. Determination stretched so tight it's about to snap.

Mephisto looks up at him the way you'd look at a puppy blocking a doorway. Amused. Not threatened. Not even dismissive — that would require noticing him enough to dismiss. Just... entertained. The way you're entertained by something small that hasn't yet learned how small it is.
"Mr. Park." He doesn't stand. Not yet. "Your devotion is... quaint. Truly. Miss Hart is fortunate to have inspired such loyalty." A pause. "But let me be clear."
He stands.

The movement is wrong — too smooth, too fluid, like watching footage with a frame dropped. One moment he's seated. The next he's upright, straightening his tie with both hands, and my eyes can't quite track the transition.

"The contract Miss Hart signed is enforceable under the jurisdiction specified in Section 101. Attempts to circumvent it through foreign legal frameworks —" his gaze slides to the Icelandic tabs still open on Leo's screens "— constitute a breach of Schedule D, Subsection 7."

He's walking toward the door now. His shoes make no sound on my floor. I notice this the way you notice silence after music stops — the absence itself is the information. Rubber soles on hardwood shouldn't be silent. Physics shouldn't allow it. But he moves through my apartment like he's moving through something that isn't entirely my apartment anymore.
"Schedule D, Subsection 7," he continues, one hand on the doorknob, "authorizes... accelerated deductions."
He lets that hang.
Accelerated deductions.

Not just losing love. Losing it faster. Not the slow quarterly drain I've been bracing against, the metered seizure of one emotion per cycle, predictable enough to grieve. No. Accelerated. All at once. Like pulling the plug instead of watching the monitor flatline.
My stomach drops through the floor.
"I trust," Mephisto says, "we understand each other."

He opens the door. Steps through. Doesn't look back. Closes it behind him — gently, softly, the way you close a door in a hotel room you might return to. Not a slam. Not a bang. Just a soft, decisive click that says: this room is mine, and I can walk back into it whenever I want.
The silence that follows is the loudest thing I've ever heard.

I stand in the middle of my apartment. My bag is still in my hand. My folder is still inside it. My mother is waiting in an office twenty blocks south, and Mephisto just told me — without telling me — that if I walk into that office, the consequences won't fall on me.
They'll fall on her.
And on Leo.
And on whatever's left of me when the deductions accelerate.

The cold lingers. His cologne got stronger as he left — oud and that ancient thing beneath it, stone and air and something that predates the word for smell. The apartment feels smaller. Contaminated. Like he measured the walls and calculated the square footage and filed it somewhere under HART, CHLOE — RESIDENCE.

Leo turns to me. His jaw is set. His hands are balled at his sides, and I can see the tremor in them — not fear, not exactly. The aftershock of standing in front of something that looked at you like you were furniture and meant it.
"Chloe." His voice is rough. "He just—"
"I know what he just."
I pick up my bag.

The folder is inside. The contract is inside. My handwriting — the timeline, the evidence, the ask — is inside. Mom is inside her office. Mephisto is inside the city, everywhere and nowhere, a contract clause with legs.
And I am standing in my apartment with forty minutes until my meeting, and my choices are: stay scared, or go.
"Chloe, you can't just — he threatened accelerated deductions. He threatened your *mom*. You need to think about—"

"I've been thinking." I shoulder my bag. "I've been thinking my whole damn life, Leo. I thought before I signed. I thought before every payment he's taken. I thought myself into a corner so small the only thing left to feel is scared, and I can't even feel *that* right."
Leo stares at me.
"He threatened my mother," I say. "That's not a warning. That's a declaration of war."
I open the door.
The hallway is empty. He's gone. Of course he's gone. He doesn't need to stay. He's already said everything — and left everything unsaid that matters more.
I step through.
The meeting is in thirty-seven minutes. Mephisto knows my strategy. He knows my ally. He knows my mother's career, my friend's debts, my vulnerabilities laid out like a map he colored in himself.
But he doesn't know I'm coming anyway.
And by the time he figures that out, I'll already be in the room.
